---
title: "Overview and workflow"
subtitle: "The typed path from dataset to first-level fit, contrast, and group summary."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

## The Spine

`fmrimod` runs one typed workflow from data to group results. Every
arrow below is a typed Python value, not a string or a hand-built matrix:

```text
fmri_dataset -> fmri_lm(spec) -> contrast -> group
```

This page walks the first-level segment on a small example with a known
injected effect; the [golden path](golden-path.qmd) carries the same
workflow all the way into the group stage.

## Dataset

An event table plus an acquisition grid, bound together in an
`fmri_dataset`.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.spec import hrf
from fmrimod.contrast import condition

events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": np.arange(8, 210, 12.0),
    "stim_type": np.where(np.arange(17) % 2 == 0, "face", "scene"),
    "duration": 2.0,
})
```

We use simulated data with a **known** face−scene effect of `2.0` in
five of ten voxels. The point of working with simulated data here is
that we know the right answer: a correct fit must return ≈ `2.0` in
those voxels and ≈ `0` in the other five.

```{python}
spec = hrf("stim_type", basis="spmg1")
```

`dataset_with_effect` is a tutorial helper that builds the simulated
data with the known effect placed on the `condition("face", ...)`
column — the same object the contrast below tests, so what we inject and
what we measure are the same condition by construction.

```{python}
from fmrimod.simulate.demo import dataset_with_effect

sampling = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=220, TR=1.0)
dataset = dataset_with_effect(
    spec, events, on=condition("face", term="stim_type"),
    effect=2.0, sampling_frame=sampling, seed=42,
)
```

## Typed Spec → First-Level Fit

The spec is a typed `hrf(...)` value — one HRF-convolved regressor per
stimulus level. `fmri_lm` compiles it, adds the baseline, and returns an
inspectable fit.

```{python}
fit = fm.fmri_lm(spec, dataset)
fit  # typed FmriLmResult — coefficients, residual df, solver config
```

The realized design is readable from the fit — built for you, still
inspectable.

```{python}
list(fit.model.design_matrix(run=0).columns)
```

## Typed Contrast

The scientific comparison names conditions, not column positions.

```{python}
face_vs_scene = fit.contrast(
    condition("face", term="stim_type") - condition("scene", term="stim_type"),
    name="face_vs_scene",
)
pd.DataFrame({
    "estimate": face_vs_scene.estimate.round(3),
    "t": face_vs_scene.stat.round(2),
    "p": face_vs_scene.p_value.round(4),
})  # voxels 0–4 recover the injected ≈ 2.0; voxels 5–9 are null
```

The estimate returns the effect we injected. Recovering the planted
value (not just *some* value) is the evidence that the design, the fit,
and the contrast weights are all specified correctly.

## Closing the Spine

The final arrow — carrying these per-subject `ContrastResult` objects
into a second-level (group) model to ask whether the effect holds across
subjects — is shown end to end, with a check against Nilearn, on the
**[golden path](golden-path.qmd)**. We stop at the first level here so
each stage stays small enough to read; the golden path is the same
workflow run without a break.

## Where to go next

The same dataset-and-spec pattern carries through to the rest of the
modeling stack: alternative GLM engines, AR whitening for temporally
correlated noise, single-trial estimation, and ready-made benchmark
datasets. Each is one option on the same `fmri_lm` call rather than a
separate API to learn.

## Next

- [The golden path](golden-path.qmd) — the whole workflow into group, with a parity check
- [Linear model](linear-model.qmd)
- [Contrasts](contrasts.qmd)
- [Group analysis](group-analysis.qmd)
